questions.question: Which U.S. president, who served from 1809 to 1817, famously declared war on Britain in the War of 1812 and is sometimes called 'the Father of the Constitution'?
questions.explanation: James Madison served as the 4th U.S. President (1809-1817) and is often called the 'Father of the Constitution' for his pivotal role in drafting it. He declared war on Britain in 1812 — the only time the U.S. has been invaded by a foreign power since independence.
questions.memory_hook: Madison = 4th president, War of 1812, Father of the Constitution.

questions.question: The 'Cultural Revolution' in China was led by whom?
questions.explanation: The Cultural Revolution (1966–1976) was launched by Mao Zedong to preserve communist ideology and eliminate perceived capitalist and traditional elements. It resulted in massive social upheaval, persecution of intellectuals, destruction of cultural artifacts, and an estimated 500,000 to 2 million deaths.
questions.memory_hook: Mao's Cultural Revolution: books burned, scholars humiliated, chaos unleashed.

questions.question: In American football, what is a 'Hail Mary' pass?
options: A running play from near the end zone
A long forward pass made in desperation at the end of a game
A trick play involving a lateral
A punt that lands in the end zone
questions.answer: A long forward pass made in desperation at the end of a game
questions.explanation: A Hail Mary is a long forward pass thrown in desperation near the end of a game, when a team is unlikely to score otherwise. The term became popular after Roger Staubach used it to describe his 50-yard touchdown pass to Drew Pearson with 24 seconds left in a 1975 NFL playoff game.
questions.memory_hook: Hail Mary: pray and throw. Roger Staubach literally named it after the prayer.

questions.question: What country does tennis player Rafael Nadal represent?
questions.explanation: Rafael Nadal is from Manacor, Mallorca, Spain. He is considered one of the greatest tennis players of all time, particularly dominant on clay courts. He has won 22 Grand Slam singles titles, including a record 14 French Open titles. He is often called 'The King of Clay.'
questions.memory_hook: Nadal = Spain = clay courts = the French Open 14 times.

questions.question: The 'Ironman Triathlon' consists of swimming, cycling, and running. What is the swimming distance?
questions.explanation: The Ironman Triathlon consists of a 2.4-mile (3.86 km) swim, a 112-mile (180.25 km) bicycle ride, and a 26.22-mile (42.2 km) marathon run, raced in that order. Athletes must complete the course within 17 hours. The course was designed to settle an argument between athletes about which sport was hardest.
questions.memory_hook: Ironman: 2.4 swim + 112 bike + 26.2 run. A day-long test of everything.

questions.question: What rapper's real name is Marshall Bruce Mathers III?
questions.explanation: Eminem's full real name is Marshall Bruce Mathers III. He grew up in Detroit, Michigan, and became one of the best-selling music artists of all time. His album 'The Marshall Mathers LP' (2000) is the fastest-selling rap album in history. He's won 15 Grammy Awards.
questions.memory_hook: Eminem = M&M = Marshall Mathers. His initials are the candy.

questions.question: Which film features the villain Hannibal Lecter in three movies?
options: Silence of the Lambs, Hannibal, and Red Dragon
questions.answer: Silence of the Lambs, Hannibal, and Red Dragon
questions.explanation: Hannibal Lecter appeared in The Silence of the Lambs (1991), Hannibal (2001), and Red Dragon (2002), all played by Anthony Hopkins. Brian Cox first played the character in Manhunter (1986), based on the same novel as Red Dragon. Hopkins' performance is widely considered one of cinema's greatest villain portrayals.
questions.memory_hook: Hopkins played Lecter three times: Silence, Hannibal, Red Dragon.

questions.question: Which British punk band, formed in London in 1975, released 'Never Mind the Bollocks, Here's the Sex Pistols' and defined the punk rock movement?
questions.explanation: The Sex Pistols formed in London in 1975 and released their only studio album 'Never Mind the Bollocks, Here's the Sex Pistols' in 1977. Though they existed for only about two years, they are widely credited with launching the British punk movement and influencing countless bands.
questions.memory_hook: Sex Pistols = British punk, 1977. One album, huge impact.

questions.question: What was the primary purpose of the Berlin Wall?
options: To keep East Germans from fleeing to the West
To mark the border of two countries
questions.answer: To keep East Germans from fleeing to the West
questions.explanation: The Berlin Wall was built by East Germany beginning on August 13, 1961, to stop the mass emigration of East Germans to West Germany through West Berlin. In the decade before the Wall, about 3.5 million East Germans had fled to the West. The Wall fell on November 9, 1989.
questions.memory_hook: The Wall kept people IN, not out — East Germans weren't allowed to leave.

questions.question: Who was the first African American to serve as U.S. Secretary of State?
questions.explanation: Colin Powell became the first African American Secretary of State when he was appointed by President George W. Bush in 2001. He served until 2005. Condoleezza Rice succeeded him as the second African American (and first African American woman) to hold the office.
questions.memory_hook: Powell = first Black Secretary of State. Condoleezza followed. Two firsts in a row.

questions.question: Which country has the most neighboring countries, sharing borders with 14 nations?
questions.explanation: China shares land borders with 14 countries: Russia, Mongolia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Afghanistan, Pakistan, India, Nepal, Bhutan, Myanmar, Laos, Vietnam, and North Korea. Russia also borders 14 countries (sharing that record), but China's neighbors span a remarkable geographic range from Central Asia to Southeast Asia.
questions.memory_hook: China and Russia each border 14 countries. China's neighbors range from Russia to Vietnam, Central Asia to South Asia. It sits at the crossroads of the continent.

questions.question: The boiling point of water at sea level is:
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius (212 degrees Fahrenheit) at sea level, where atmospheric pressure is 101.3 kPa. At higher altitudes, where atmospheric pressure is lower, water boils at a lower temperature — which is why cooking instructions differ for high-altitude locations. In a pressure cooker, water boils above 100 degrees.
questions.memory_hook: Water boils at 100 C at sea level. Higher up the mountain, it boils at lower temperatures. Lower pressure = lower boiling point. That's why pasta takes longer to cook at altitude.
